This means clicking on purple with white at (11,34) did NOT remove (11,34). The white border just moved to the HUD again.
So the pattern is:
- Clicking on purple removes the white-bordered object ONLY if the white-bordered object is adjacent to the purple object on the trail.
- (23,37) was adjacent to purple on the trail, so it was removed.
- (11,34) is NOT adjacent to purple, so it was not removed.
Let me check which bordered objects are adjacent to purple. Looking at the trail:
- (11,34) - top
- (23,37) - upper middle (was adjacent to purple, now removed)
- Orange (31,36) - junction
- Purple (35,38) - lower middle
- (42,40) - lower (adjacent to purple?)
- (51,40) - bottom
So (42,40) might be adjacent to purple. Let me try moving white to (42,40) and clicking on purple.
But wait, I need to understand the adjacency better. Let me check the current segmentation to see which objects are adjacent to purple.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check adjacency to purple object
seg = current_frame.segmentation
purple_id = None
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] > 5:
        purple_id = n['id']
        break

print(f"Purple node id: {purple_id}")
print("Adjacent to purple:")
for adj in seg['adjacency_list']:
    if purple_id in adj:
        other = adj[0] if adj[1] == purple_id else adj[1]
        other_node = seg['nodes'][other]
        cr = sum(b[0] for b in other_node['boundary']) // len(other_node['boundary'])
        cc = sum(b[1] for b in other_node['boundary']) // len(other_node['boundary'])
        print(f"  Node {other}: color={other_node['color']} pixels={other_node['pixels']} (~{cr},{cc})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
